title: Detecting Outliers: Z-Score
concept: The Z-score measures how many standard deviations a data point is from the mean, flagging values beyond three steps as outliers.

exercises.prompt: Warmup: Why do extreme outliers distort the mean (average) but not the median?

options.text: The mean sums all values including the outlier, while the median only looks at the ordered middle value
The median dynamically deletes values larger than 100
The mean is only used for categorical variables

exercises.explain: Exactly! Because the mean includes every value in its sum, outliers drag it. Today, we'll learn standard ways to mathematically flag these outliers.

cards.title: Percentiles and growth charts
cards.body: When doctors track a child's height, they check standard deviations above or below the average. They're using standard deviation steps to see if a value is normal or extreme.

cards.title: What is a Z-score?
cards.body: A Z-score measures how many standard deviations a data point is from the mean. A Z-score of 0 is exactly average, while positive or negative scores show how far it is from the center.

cards.title: Straying from the path
cards.body: Imagine walking down a path. Each standard deviation is a single stride. If most people stay within 3 steps of the center line, someone who is 5 steps away is a clear outlier.

cards.title: Test Score Deviations
cards.body: If average test score is 70 with a standard deviation of 10, a score of 100 is 3 standard deviations above the mean (Z-score of +3). A score of 40 is 3 standard deviations below (Z-score of -3).

cards.title: Z-score has assumptions
cards.body: Can you use Z-score on any dataset? Not quite! Z-scores assume your data fits a normal distribution (bell curve). If your data is highly skewed, Z-score might flag normal points as outliers.

cards.title: The Z-Score Threshold
cards.body: How Z-scores partition data in a standard bell curve distribution:

left.text:
Normal Range
Contains 99.7% of data
Considered standard data

right.text:
Outlier Zone
Contains 0.3% of data
Flagged as anomalous

exercises.prompt: What does a Z-score of -2.5 tell you about a data point?

options.text: It is 2.5 standard deviations below the mean
It is 2.5 times the median value
It is exactly 2.5 units above the mean

exercises.explain: A negative Z-score indicates the value is below the mean, and its magnitude represents the number of standard deviations away.

exercises.prompt: True or false: The Z-score method is highly effective for detecting outliers in highly skewed, non-bell-shaped distributions.
exercises.explain: False! Z-score relies on mean and standard deviation, which are themselves distorted by skewness, making it less reliable for skewed data.

exercises.sentence: The standard threshold to flag an outlier using Z-score is a value greater than ___ or less than -3.
exercises.explain: In a normal distribution, 99.7% of data lies within 3 standard deviations, so anything beyond 3 or -3 is standardly considered an outlier.

exercises.prompt: Which of the following values has the highest likelihood of being flagged as an outlier using the standard Z-score method?
exercises.explain: A Z-score of +4.2 is well beyond the typical threshold of +3, meaning it is extremely far from the average.

exercises.prompt: You are building a sensor monitoring system for a water pump. The normal operating temperature is 150°F with a standard deviation of 5°F. The current reading is 170°F. What is its Z-score, and should it be flagged as an anomaly?

options.text: Z-score is +4.0. It should be flagged as an outlier because it is beyond the standard threshold of +3.
Z-score is -4.0. It is too cold.

exercises.explain: Correct! The Z-score is calculated as (170 - 150) / 5 = 20 / 5 = +4.0. Since this is greater than the standard threshold of +3, it is highly anomalous. Spectacular job mastering Z-scores!
